seo.title: Healthcare Data Extraction AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for data extraction AI agents in healthcare. Covers HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, HITECH Act.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for data extraction in healthcare environments carries critical compliance obligations under HIPAA, HITECH, and FDA 21 CFR Part 11, where a single misconfiguration can expose Protected Health Information (PHI) and trigger fines up to $1.5M per violation. Structured output extraction from clinical documents—such as discharge summaries, lab reports, and EHR notes—requires rigorous pre-processing pipelines to de-identify PHI before any LLM interaction. This checklist ensures your extraction architecture meets regulatory requirements, maintains full auditability, and protects patient safety at every stage of the pipeline.

sections.heading: PHI De-identification & Data Governance
sections.description: Before any unstructured document reaches an LLM, PHI must be systematically identified, redacted, or tokenized. This section covers the mandatory data handling controls required under HIPAA Safe Harbor and Expert Determination methods.

items.label: Implement HIPAA Safe Harbor De-identification
items.description: Remove all 18 HIPAA Safe Harbor identifiers (names, dates, geographic data, phone numbers, SSNs, MRNs, etc.) from source documents using a validated NER-based redaction pipeline before passing text to any LLM endpoint.

items.label: Deploy a Pre-LLM PHI Scrubbing Layer
items.description: Integrate a dedicated PHI detection service (e.g., AWS Comprehend Medical, Microsoft Presidio, or an on-premise equivalent) as a mandatory gate in the extraction pipeline. Block any document that fails scrubbing from proceeding.

items.label: Enforce Data Residency — No PHI Leaves the Processing Environment
items.description: Configure all LLM API calls to use private endpoints (VPC-peered or on-premise models). Explicitly prohibit routing PHI-containing payloads to third-party public LLM APIs. Document network topology and enforce via firewall rules.

items.label: Tokenize Patient Identifiers for Internal Traceability
items.description: Replace PHI fields with reversible tokens (e.g., patient_id → TKN-8472) before extraction. Maintain a secure token-to-PHI mapping in an isolated, access-controlled vault to allow re-linkage only by authorized personnel post-extraction.

items.label: Classify Document Sensitivity Before Processing
items.description: Automatically tag each incoming document with a sensitivity classification (e.g., PHI-present, PHI-absent, research-de-identified) using a pre-processing classifier. Route documents to appropriate processing pipelines based on classification.

items.label: Establish Data Retention and Purge Policies for Intermediate Artifacts
items.description: Define maximum retention windows for raw documents, de-identified intermediates, LLM prompts, and extracted outputs. Automate purge schedules and log all deletion events to comply with HIPAA minimum necessary and HITECH breach notification rules.

items.label: Conduct Quarterly De-identification Accuracy Audits
items.description: Run a sample of 200+ documents through the de-identification pipeline and manually verify PHI removal accuracy. Target a false-negative rate below 0.1%. Document audit results and remediation actions for regulatory review.

sections.heading: Schema Validation & Extraction Accuracy
sections.description: Structured output extraction must produce schema-compliant, type-consistent, and clinically accurate results. Errors in extracted fields—such as incorrect medication dosages or misattributed diagnoses—can directly cause patient harm.

items.label: Define and Version-Control Extraction Schemas
items.description: Maintain all extraction schemas (e.g., JSON Schema or Pydantic models) in a version-controlled repository. Each schema must specify field names, data types, required vs. optional status, allowed value ranges, and clinical coding standards (ICD-10, SNOMED CT, LOINC).

items.label: Enforce Strict Schema Compliance on Every LLM Output
items.description: Parse every LLM response against the target schema using a validation library (e.g., Pydantic, Zod, jsonschema). Reject and flag any output that fails type checks, missing required fields, or violates enum constraints. Never pass unvalidated output downstream.

items.label: Implement Field-Level Confidence Scoring
items.description: Require the LLM to output a confidence score (0–1) for each extracted field alongside the value. Flag records where any critical field (e.g., medication name, dosage, diagnosis code) scores below 0.85 for human review before clinical use.

items.label: Validate Extracted Values Against Clinical Reference Databases
items.description: Cross-check extracted medication names against RxNorm, diagnosis codes against ICD-10-CM, and lab values against LOINC reference ranges. Reject extractions that produce codes not present in the reference database.

items.label: Run Completeness Validation on Required Clinical Fields
items.description: After extraction, verify that all mandatory fields defined in the schema (e.g., patient encounter date, primary diagnosis, prescribing provider) are populated. Generate a completeness score per record and route incomplete records to a review queue.

items.label: Establish a Ground-Truth Test Set for Regression Testing
items.description: Maintain a labeled dataset of 500+ de-identified clinical documents with verified extraction outputs. Run regression tests against this dataset on every model or prompt change. Require F1 score ≥ 0.95 on critical fields before deploying updates.

items.label: Handle Ambiguous or Conflicting Extracted Values Explicitly
items.description: Define a conflict resolution protocol for cases where the LLM extracts contradictory values (e.g., two different dosages for the same medication). Route conflicting records to a clinician reviewer rather than auto-resolving with heuristics.

sections.heading: Audit Logging & FDA 21 CFR Part 11 Compliance
sections.description: All AI-assisted data extraction decisions must be fully auditable under FDA 21 CFR Part 11 and HIPAA. This section covers the logging, electronic signature, and audit trail requirements for regulated healthcare AI systems.

items.label: Log Every Extraction Event with Full Provenance
items.description: For each extraction run, log: document ID, timestamp (UTC), model version, prompt template version, schema version, extracted output hash, validation result, confidence scores, and the identity of the initiating user or system. Store logs in a tamper-evident, append-only store.

items.label: Implement Electronic Signatures for Clinician Review Actions
items.description: When a clinician approves, rejects, or modifies an extracted record, capture an electronic signature per FDA 21 CFR Part 11 §11.50 requirements: user ID, timestamp, and the meaning of the signature (e.g., 'reviewed and approved'). Bind signatures cryptographically to the record.

items.label: Maintain Immutable Audit Trails for All Data Modifications
items.description: Ensure that any modification to an extracted record creates a new versioned entry rather than overwriting the original. Audit trails must capture who changed what, when, and why. Use a write-once storage backend (e.g., AWS WORM S3, Azure Immutable Blob Storage).

items.label: Enforce Role-Based Access Control on Audit Logs
items.description: Restrict audit log access to authorized compliance officers, auditors, and system administrators. Log all access to audit logs themselves. Prevent developers and data scientists from modifying or deleting log entries.

items.label: Generate Automated Compliance Reports for Regulatory Submissions
items.description: Build automated reporting pipelines that produce HIPAA audit reports, 21 CFR Part 11 compliance summaries, and SOC 2 evidence packages on a scheduled basis. Reports must include extraction volume, error rates, human review rates, and PHI incident counts.

items.label: Set Audit Log Retention to Minimum 7 Years
items.description: Configure log retention policies to retain all extraction audit logs for at least 7 years to satisfy HIPAA record retention requirements and FDA 21 CFR Part 11 §11.10(e). Implement automated archival to cold storage after 2 years with guaranteed retrieval SLAs.

items.label: Test Audit Trail Integrity Monthly
items.description: Run automated integrity checks on audit log stores monthly to detect tampering, gaps, or corruption. Use cryptographic hash chaining or a blockchain-anchored audit log to provide verifiable proof of log integrity for regulatory inspections.

sections.heading: Human-in-the-Loop Controls & Incident Response
sections.description: Given the critical sensitivity of healthcare data extraction, automated outputs must be subject to defined human oversight thresholds, and the system must have a tested incident response plan for extraction failures, PHI breaches, and model degradation.

items.label: Define Mandatory Human Review Thresholds for Clinical Use
items.description: Establish explicit rules requiring human clinician review before extracted data is used in clinical decisions: any record with a field confidence score below 0.85, any record with validation failures, and 100% of records in high-stakes contexts (e.g., medication orders, surgical planning).

items.label: Implement a PHI Breach Detection and HITECH Notification Workflow
items.description: Deploy real-time monitoring to detect if PHI reaches an unauthorized endpoint (e.g., a public LLM API, an unencrypted log). Trigger an automated incident response workflow that notifies the Privacy Officer within 1 hour and initiates the HITECH 60-day breach notification process.

items.label: Establish Model Performance Degradation Alerts
items.description: Monitor extraction accuracy metrics (F1 score, completeness rate, schema validation pass rate) in real time. Alert the ML engineering team and pause automated processing if any critical metric drops more than 5% below the validated baseline within a 24-hour window.

items.label: Create a Documented Rollback Procedure for Model and Prompt Updates
items.description: Maintain the last two validated versions of the extraction model and prompt templates in a deployable state. Document a step-by-step rollback procedure that can be executed within 30 minutes. Test rollback procedures quarterly.

items.label: Train Clinical and Operations Staff on AI Extraction Limitations
items.description: Conduct mandatory training for all staff who interact with AI-extracted data, covering: known failure modes, how to identify suspicious extractions, the escalation process for errors, and the prohibition on using unreviewed AI output for clinical decisions.

items.label: Conduct Bi-Annual Penetration Testing of the Extraction Pipeline
items.description: Engage a third-party security firm to perform penetration testing on the full extraction pipeline every 6 months, specifically targeting PHI exfiltration vectors, prompt injection attacks, and unauthorized access to audit logs. Remediate all critical findings within 30 days.

items.label: Document and Test a Business Continuity Plan for Extraction System Outages
items.description: Define manual fallback procedures for clinical data extraction workflows in the event of AI system unavailability. Test the fallback process annually and ensure clinical operations can continue without AI-assisted extraction for at least 72 hours.

notes.note: Under 45 CFR §164.514, PHI must be de-identified using either the Safe Harbor method (removing all 18 identifiers) or Expert Determination (statistical verification) before processing with any LLM. HITECH extends HIPAA breach notification obligations to Business Associates—ensure your AI vendor has a signed BAA and that your extraction pipeline is scoped within the BAA's permitted uses. Violations carry tiered penalties from $100 to $50,000 per violation, capped at $1.5M per violation category per year.

If extracted data is used in FDA-regulated activities (clinical trials, drug manufacturing, medical device records), the extraction system qualifies as an electronic records system subject to 21 CFR Part 11. This requires: validated software (IQ/OQ/PQ documentation), audit trails that cannot be disabled, electronic signatures with user authentication, and system access controls. The LLM model version and prompt templates must be treated as validated software components—any change requires re-validation.

SOC 2 Type II certification requires demonstrating that security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy controls are operating effectively over a minimum 6-month observation period. For AI extraction pipelines, this means continuous monitoring of access controls, encryption in transit and at rest, change management procedures for model updates, and incident response testing. Ensure your extraction infrastructure vendor provides a current SOC 2 Type II report and that your own controls are documented for your auditor.

notes.regulation: State-Level Privacy Laws (CCPA, NY SHIELD, Texas HB 300)
notes.note: Beyond federal HIPAA requirements, several states impose additional obligations on health data processors. Texas HB 300 extends HIPAA-equivalent protections to all covered entities handling PHI of Texas residents, with independent state penalties. California's CMIA and CCPA grant patients rights to access and delete their data, which must be honored even for AI-processed records. Audit your patient population geography and ensure your extraction pipeline's data subject rights workflows (access, correction, deletion) are functional and tested.

cta.heading: Automate Your Healthcare Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your healthcare AI extraction pipeline against HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, and HITECH requirements—catching compliance gaps before they become $1.5M violations.
